text.title: Fitness Functions as Code Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules to encode architecture invariants as runnable tests so quarterly review compares green-bar diffs over time and drift-detection has machine signal, not opinion.

rule.statement: The architecture-as-code repo includes a `fitness/` directory with one runnable test per invariant, written in the language-native framework: ArchUnit (JVM), ts-arch or dependency-cruiser (TypeScript), import-linter (Python), depgraph or go-cleanarch (Go), Roslyn analyzers (C#). One framework per language — never two competing frameworks for the same module.

rationale.source: Geek/sdlc-ai gap: rules must be runnable, not prose

rule.rationale: Prose architecture rules cannot be executed; choosing exactly one ecosystem-native framework per language gives AI agents and humans a single check command and avoids contradictory rule sets.

rule.statement: Layering and dependency-direction predicates are encoded as positive allow-lists, not negative deny-lists. The fitness test states "module `domain` may depend on `core` only"; any other import fails the test. The allow-list is the single source of truth for the dependency graph.
rule.rationale: Deny-lists rot quietly as new modules appear, because the absence of a rule defaults to allow. Allow-lists fail loudly on every novel import, which is exactly what drift detection needs.

rule.statement: Performance budgets are encoded as runnable assertions, not commentary: hot-path functions carry an annotation (`@PerfBudget(p99_ms=50)`) or a sibling `.perf.yaml`, and a CI test executes the hot-path microbenchmark and fails the build when the budget is breached. Budgets without an executable assertion are deleted from the repo.
rule.rationale: A budget written in a wiki page is decorative. A budget that fails a build forces a real conversation about either fixing the regression or revising the budget — and that conversation is logged in the PR.

rule.statement: License and supply-chain invariants are encoded as fitness tests too: an SPDX allow-list test fails on any introduced dependency outside the allowed list; a "no `@Deprecated` in public API" test fails on regression. These run on every PR alongside layering tests, not as a separate quarterly audit.
rule.rationale: Pushing licence and deprecation checks into the same fitness suite means they share the same break-build-or-pass discipline as architectural layering — compliance stops being a once-a-quarter scramble.

rule.statement: Quarterly architecture review consumes a machine-generated drift report built from the fitness suite's git history: which invariants were added, which were skipped (`@Ignore` / `@xfail`), and which thresholds were widened. Any invariant marked "temporarily skipped" must have an expiry date inside the same review; expired skips block the next quarter's review approval.

rationale.source: Quarterly review + drift detection depend on encoded rules

rule.rationale: A quarterly review without a machine diff is theatre. The skip-with-expiry mechanism is the only one that prevents permanent silencing of expensive-to-fix invariants.

example.title: Layering invariant in ArchUnit + perf budget in pytest
example.note: Every architectural commitment is either a green CI line or an explicit, dated waiver. AI-generated PRs that cross a layer boundary fail on submission, not at quarterly review.
